topic: Trade in the Contemporary World / Global Migration / Human Rights / Power & Borders
subject: geography

items.q: Is trade between countries a zero-sum game?
items.correct: No — comparative advantage means both partners can gain from specialisation and exchange, though the gains are often shared unevenly between and within countries.
items.misconception: If one country gains from trade, its partner must lose.
items.random_wrong: Countries take turns winning at trade each year.
items.why: Zero-sum thinking blocks understanding of why trade grows and of who really wins and loses.

items.q: Do most migrants move from the world's poorest countries to the richest?
items.correct: No — much migration is regional or between developing countries, and the very poorest often cannot afford to move far; emigration commonly rises as incomes initially grow.
items.misconception: Migration flows simply run from the poorest nations to the richest.
items.why: The migration hump and regional flows correct the simple poverty-push model students assume.

items.q: Does state sovereignty mean borders are fixed forever?
items.correct: No — borders change through treaties, conflict and independence movements, and sovereignty is increasingly contested by supranational bodies, TNCs and global flows.
items.misconception: National borders are permanent natural features.
items.random_wrong: Borders are repainted every decade by the UN.
items.why: The power and borders theme asks how sovereignty is challenged; static views prevent that analysis.
The answer examiners want is: McLean. “Bezos” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What does the KOF Index measure?
items.why: It's Globalisation. “Poverty” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Top 500 TNCs control roughly what percentage of trade?
items.why: The real figure is 70 %. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
